**Giuseppe Di Vittorio** (11 August 1892 – 3 November 1957), also known as **Mario Nicoletti**, was an Italian [trade union](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") leader and [communist](/wiki/Communist "Communist") politician.

He was one of the most influential trade union leaders of the [labour movement](/wiki/Labour_movement "Labour movement") after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[[1]](#cite_note-Di_Vittorio-1)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] He became president of the [World Federation of Trade Unions](/wiki/World_Federation_of_Trade_Unions "World Federation of Trade Unions").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giuseppe_Di_Vittorio&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Giuseppe Di Vittorio was born in [Cerignola](/wiki/Cerignola "Cerignola"), [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia"), into a family of poor agricultural day laborers. After his father's death, Di Vittorio was forced to leave school and work as a day laborer. He joined the May 1904 general strike during which five workers were killed by troops in Cerignola. Di Vittorio was strongly influenced by the growth of peasants' organizations and the spread of socialist ideas, giving rise to his participation in the local young socialist organization in Cerignola. He was radicalised by affiliating with the national Federazione Giovanile Socialista (Federation of Young Socialists), led by [syndicalists](/wiki/Syndicalists "Syndicalists") in opposition to the official youth federation of the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party") (PSI). His involvement in the socialist and labour movement grew, and 1911 became chairman of the [Camera del Lavoro](/wiki/Camera_del_Lavoro "Camera del Lavoro") in [Minervino Murge](/wiki/Minervino_Murge "Minervino Murge").

As a native of the *[Mezzogiorno](/wiki/Mezzogiorno "Mezzogiorno")*, Di Vittorio became involved in the union plans for solving the region's acute problems in the manner illustrated by the [Fasci Siciliani](/wiki/Fasci_Siciliani "Fasci Siciliani") (Sicilian Workers Leagues) in the final decade of the 19th century. A partisan of [insurgence](/wiki/Insurgence "Insurgence"), Di Vittorio became a leader of an anarcho-syndicalist [Unione Sindacale Italiana](/wiki/Unione_Sindacale_Italiana "Unione Sindacale Italiana") (USI), after its formation in 1912. After the [Red Week](/wiki/Red_Week_(Italy) "Red Week (Italy)"), a number of arrest warrants were issued against him, leading to his escape to [Lugano](/wiki/Lugano "Lugano"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), in June 1914; he would return ten months later, when the government issued a general amnesty.[[4]](#cite_note-rassegna-4)

While the majority of the USI opposed [militarism](/wiki/Militarism "Militarism"), [Randolfo Pacciardi](/wiki/Randolfo_Pacciardi "Randolfo Pacciardi") claimed that Di Vittorio belonged to a minority of left-wingers, like [Alceste De Ambris](/wiki/Alceste_De_Ambris "Alceste De Ambris") and [Filippo Corridoni](/wiki/Filippo_Corridoni "Filippo Corridoni"), who supported [irredentist claims](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_during_World_War_I "Italian irredentism during World War I") and advocated Italy's entry into World War I. Di Vittorio later denied this,[[4]](#cite_note-rassegna-4) but there is a pro-war article under his name appearing on *[Il Popolo d'Italia](/wiki/Il_Popolo_d%27Italia "Il Popolo d'Italia")* in June 1915.[[5]](#cite_note-5) He served in the conflict in a [Bersaglieri](/wiki/Bersaglieri "Bersaglieri") unit, and was discharged in 1916 after having been gravely wounded.[[4]](#cite_note-rassegna-4)

## Opposition to fascism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giuseppe_Di_Vittorio&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Opposition to fascism")]

In the [1921 Italian general election](/wiki/1921_Italian_general_election "1921 Italian general election"), Di Vittorio was elected to Parliament in the lists of the PSI. For the [1924 Italian general election](/wiki/1924_Italian_general_election "1924 Italian general election"), he joined the [Communist Party of Italy](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Italy "Communist Party of Italy") (PCdI) but failed to be re-elected. Di Vittorio was also a member of the militant [anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascist "Anti-fascist") organisation *[Arditi del Popolo](/wiki/Arditi_del_Popolo "Arditi del Popolo")*.

The new situation after the rise to power of [fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") and the [March on Rome](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome") made him an enemy of [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini")'s regime. In May 1927, he was sentenced *[in absentia](/wiki/Trial_in_absentia "Trial in absentia")* to twelve years of imprisonment for subversive propaganda by the [Special Tribunal for the Defense of the State](/wiki/Special_Tribunal_for_the_Defense_of_the_State "Special Tribunal for the Defense of the State").[[6]](#cite_note-6) He managed to flee to France, and later lived in the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") from 1928 to 1930, where he represented the dissolved [General Confederation of Labour](/wiki/General_Confederation_of_Labour_(Italy) "General Confederation of Labour (Italy)") in the [Profintern](/wiki/Profintern "Profintern"), as well as being Italy's representative in the [Krestintern](/wiki/Krestintern "Krestintern"). Afterwards, he returned to Paris, where he was a member of the Politburo of the Italian Communist Party.

Di Vittorio joined the [Republican](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") side fighting [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco")'s forces during the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), he was Political Commissar of the [XI International Brigade](/wiki/XI_International_Brigade "XI International Brigade"). After the fall of the Republic, he headed the board of a Paris-based antifascist newspaper, *La Voce degli Italiani*.

In 1941, Di Vittorio was arrested by German authorities in France on a request from [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy "Fascist Italy"), and held in internal exile on [Ventotene](/wiki/Ventotene "Ventotene").

## Post-war years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Giuseppe_Di_Vittorio&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Post-war years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Furriers_Reception_Given_to_Giuseppe_DiVittorio%2C_Secretary_of_the_Italian_General_Confederation_of_Labor%2C_International_Joint_Board._Oct_1946_Slide_1.png/330px-Furriers_Reception_Given_to_Giuseppe_DiVittorio%2C_Secretary_of_the_Italian_General_Confederation_of_Labor%2C_International_Joint_Board._Oct_1946_Slide_1.png)](/wiki/File:Furriers_Reception_Given_to_Giuseppe_DiVittorio,_Secretary_of_the_Italian_General_Confederation_of_Labor,_International_Joint_Board._Oct_1946_Slide_1.png)

Di Vittorio (standing) addresses a meeting of the [International Fur & Leather Workers Union](/wiki/International_Fur_%26_Leather_Workers_Union "International Fur & Leather Workers Union") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), October 1946

In 1944, Di Vittorio, along with socialist and Catholic union leaders, agreed to re-establish CGIL as a representative of all currents of trade unionism in Italy, including communists, [socialists](/wiki/Socialists "Socialists"), [Christian democrats](/wiki/Christian_democrats "Christian democrats"), and [anarcho-syndicalists](/wiki/Anarcho-syndicalists "Anarcho-syndicalists"). He was elected union secretary the following year.

As a union representative, Di Vittorio sat on the [National Council](/wiki/National_Council_(Italy) "National Council (Italy)"), an advisory body created to fulfill the role of a provisional legislature from September 1945 to June 1946. In the [1946 Italian general election](/wiki/1946_Italian_general_election "1946 Italian general election"), he was elected to the [Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly_of_Italy "Constituent Assembly of Italy") as a member of the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") (PCI), and would later be re-elected as a member of the new Parliament in the [1948](/wiki/1948_Italian_general_election "1948 Italian general election") and [1957](/wiki/1953_Italian_general_election "1953 Italian general election") Italian general elections.

With the onset of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") and the breakdown of the alliance between the main anti-fascist parties, particularly [Christian Democracy](/wiki/Christian_Democracy_(Italy) "Christian Democracy (Italy)") (DC) and the Italian Communist Party, the organisation suffered internal divisions. When a right-wing student attempted to assassinate the PCI leader [Palmiro Togliatti](/wiki/Palmiro_Togliatti "Palmiro Togliatti") in July 1948, CGIL called a general strike and organised street protests during which widespread rioting occurred. After this event, those affiliated with the Christian Democrats within the union left to establish the [Confederazione Italiana Sindacati Lavoratori](/wiki/Confederazione_Italiana_Sindacati_Lavoratori "Confederazione Italiana Sindacati Lavoratori") (CISL). They were followed in May 1949 by supporters of the [Italian Democratic Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Democratic_Socialist_Party "Italian Democratic Socialist Party"), which split from the PSI and left to form what would become the [Unione Italiana del Lavoro](/wiki/Unione_Italiana_del_Lavoro "Unione Italiana del Lavoro") (UIL). These three organisations continue to be Italy's main labour unions.

During the [Hungarian Revolution of 1956](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956 "Hungarian Revolution of 1956"), Di Vittorio clashed with Togliatti over the CGIL's statements of support for the Hungarian insurgents.[[7]](#cite_note-7) According to [Antonio Giolitti](/wiki/Antonio_Giolitti "Antonio Giolitti"), this position was supported by Di Vittorio himself during private meetings, and archival documents show that the CGIL secretary faced pressure from the PCI leadership to retract the statements.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Di Vittorio continued leading CGIL as Italy's largest union with the backing of the PCI and PSI until his death from [heart attack](/wiki/Heart_attack "Heart attack") in 1957. He was also a longtime leader of the [World Federation of Trade Unions](/wiki/World_Federation_of_Trade_Unions "World Federation of Trade Unions"). His strong charisma made him the most popular myth of the Italian workers. His funeral was attended by more than three million people coming to Rome from all over Italy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
